text:
Large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT and Gemini can answer a wide range of questions and are highly cerebral, but can easily produce “hallucinations” (i.e., made-up information) when providing their answers. The inaccuracy of LLMs is a significant problem in many domains, such as creating legal findings that do not exist, spreading misinformation in news and even in medicine, such as radiology. Training LLMs to be more truthful with constraints, or having humans correct their answers also has its limitations. Our goal is to develop a test that is universally applicable to all LLMs and does not require the user to have knowledge of the truth to the question they are asking.

We present a statistical approach to pinpointing what types of inputs elicit what types of hallucinations via the use of entropy-based uncertainty metrics for spotting “confabulations”, a particular type of hallucination characterized by the generation of entirely novel and randomly chosen outputs. This method handles linguistic uncertainty at the semantic level as opposed to the syntactical level and is shown to perform well across a wide array of datasets and applications, with no prior subject matter knowledge or need for annotated training data. With the ability to gauge the likelihood that any given input will be the source of a confabulation, users can better understand how to proceed when interacting with large language models and models in general in situations where unsafe outputs could cause real-world consequences such as sensitive information leaking out or damage being done during critical tasks such as healthcare and autonomous vehicles.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
